children.name: Fregata
children.description: The sole genus of the family, comprising five species of large, lightly built seabirds instantly recognisable by their deeply forked tails, long hooked bills, and the male's inflatable red throat pouch. Frigatebirds are masters of aerial piracy, harrying other seabirds to force them to disgorge their catch.

children.name: Fregata magnificens
children.description: The only frigatebird found regularly in the Caribbean and along the tropical Atlantic coast of the Americas. The male inflates his vivid red gular pouch into a gleaming heart-shaped balloon for hours on end, attracting passing females with low drumming calls.

children.name: Fregata minor
children.description: The most widespread frigatebird, ranging across tropical oceans worldwide except the Atlantic. Males lack the green sheen of the magnificent frigatebird and show a purple iridescence on the back during the breeding season.

children.name: Fregata ariel
children.description: The smallest frigatebird, distinguished by white axillary spurs that extend onto the flanks. Despite its petite size, it is a relentless kleptoparasite, chasing boobies and terns until they regurgitate their meals in midair.

children.name: Fregata aquila
children.description: Endemic to Ascension Island in the South Atlantic, where it breeds in just a single colony on Boatswain Bird Island. Once far more numerous, its population was decimated by introduced rats and cats on the main island.

children.name: Fregata andrewsi
children.description: One of the rarest seabirds in the world, breeding only on Christmas Island in the Indian Ocean. With fewer than 5,000 individuals remaining, it is classified as Critically Endangered, threatened by phosphate mining and habitat degradation.
